amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: E. coli heat shock response is mediated by alternative sigma factor σ32 (RpoH) which directs RNA polymerase to heat shock gene promoters. Under normal conditions, σ32 is unstable (t1/2 ~1 min) due to DnaK/DnaJ/GrpE chaperones targeting it for FtsH protease degradation. Heat stress causes protein misfolding, titrating away chaperones. Free σ32 accumulates, transcribes ~30 heat shock genes including DnaK/J, GroEL/ES, ClpB, Lon, FtsH. This creates negative feedback as newly synthesized chaperones re-sequester σ32.

name: Heat Shock Response and σ32 (RpoH) Regulation
notes: Classic example of post-translational regulation via protein stability. Shows negative feedback loop.
scientificAccuracy: Based on comprehensive research on E. coli heat shock response.

sources.title: The RpoH protein of E. coli, a sigma-32 homolog, is required for induction of heat shock proteins.
sources.authors: Grossman AD, Erickson JW, Gross CA.

sources.title: DnaK, DnaJ, and GrpE heat shock proteins interact with RNA polymerase sigma 32 to control the initiation of heat shock gene expression.
sources.authors: Straus DB, Walter WA, Gross CA.

sources.title: Degradation of sigma 32 by the ATP-dependent FtsH protease is required for bacterial viability.
sources.authors: Herman C, Thevenet D, D'Ari R, Bouloc P.
